question_text: Which of the following is a well-known advantage of circuit training that makes it popular in school Physical Education programmes with large groups of students?

options.text: It requires each student to have their own personal trainer supervising them individually
It allows a large number of students to exercise simultaneously at different stations, making efficient use of limited time, space and equipment
It can only be performed by students who have prior experience in competitive sports
It requires a fully equipped gymnasium with expensive weight machines at every station

explanation: Circuit training is ideal for school PE because multiple students can exercise simultaneously at different stations using minimal equipment (bodyweight exercises, skipping ropes, medicine balls). This makes efficient use of limited class time, space and equipment — a major practical advantage for large groups.
elimination_hints: A is impractical for schools; C excludes beginners (circuit is suitable for all levels); D is false (bodyweight exercises work).
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: In weight training, the terms 'repetitions' and 'sets' are fundamental to programme design. Which of the following correctly defines both terms and illustrates their relationship?

options.text: A repetition is one complete execution of an exercise, and a set is a group of consecutive repetitions; for example, performing twelve bicep curls consecutively constitutes one set of twelve repetitions
A set is one single lift of the weight, while a repetition refers to the total number of sets performed
Repetitions refer to the number of different exercises in a session, while sets refer to the rest periods between exercises
Both terms are identical in meaning and can be used interchangeably in any training context

explanation: A repetition (rep) is one complete execution of an exercise movement (e.g., one bicep curl from start to finish). A set is a group of consecutive repetitions performed without rest. For example, 3 sets of 12 reps means the athlete performs 12 consecutive curls (one set), rests, and repeats this two more times.
elimination_hints: B reverses the definitions; C redefines both terms incorrectly; D claims they are identical which they are not.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: A Physical Education teacher designs a circuit with twelve stations where students alternate between upper-body and lower-body exercises at consecutive stations. Analyze the primary reason behind this alternating arrangement.

options.text: It is arranged randomly and the alternating pattern has no particular physiological purpose

Alternating muscle groups allows one set of muscles to recover while the other set works, enabling the student to maintain exercise quality and intensity throughout the circuit without premature local muscle fatigue
It is done only to make the circuit look visually organised when the inspector visits the school
Upper-body exercises are always easier than lower-body exercises so they serve as rest stations

explanation: Alternating upper-body and lower-body exercises at consecutive stations is a deliberate design principle. While the upper body works at one station, the lower body recovers (and vice versa). This prevents premature local muscular fatigue in any single muscle group, allowing students to maintain exercise quality and intensity throughout the entire circuit.
elimination_hints: A denies the intentional design; C is trivial; D incorrectly categorises upper-body exercises as universally easier.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: Evaluate the statement: 'Weight training is unsuitable for adolescents and should be avoided entirely in school Physical Education programmes because it stunts growth and causes injury.' Is this statement supported by current sports science evidence?

options.text: Yes, the statement is fully supported — weight training permanently damages growth plates in all adolescents

No, current evidence indicates that properly supervised weight training with appropriate loads and correct technique is safe and beneficial for adolescents, improving strength, bone density and injury resilience without stunting growth
Yes, adolescents should only perform aerobic exercises until they reach the age of twenty-five
No, but only because weight training has no effect on the body before the age of eighteen

explanation: Current sports science evidence from organisations like the American Academy of Pediatrics and NSCA confirms that properly supervised weight training with age-appropriate loads and correct technique is safe and beneficial for adolescents. It improves muscular strength, bone mineral density and injury resilience. The myth of growth stunting is not supported by evidence when proper guidelines are followed.
elimination_hints: A perpetuates a debunked myth; C sets an arbitrary age (25) with no scientific basis; D incorrectly claims weight training has no pre-18 effect.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: Which of the following correctly explains why dynamic stretching is preferred over static stretching during the warm-up phase before explosive sports activities like sprinting and jumping?

options.text: Dynamic stretching is preferred because it is less effective than static stretching and therefore safer for warm-ups

Dynamic stretching involves active movements that take joints through their full range of motion, increasing blood flow, activating muscles and improving neuromuscular readiness, whereas prolonged static stretching before explosive activities may temporarily reduce muscle power output
Static stretching is always superior and should be performed before every type of physical activity without exception
Dynamic stretching and static stretching produce identical physiological effects during warm-up

explanation: Dynamic stretching uses controlled active movements (leg swings, arm circles, walking lunges) that increase blood flow, activate the neuromuscular system and improve range of motion. Research shows prolonged static stretching before explosive activities can temporarily reduce power output and sprint performance, making dynamic stretching the preferred warm-up choice for explosive sports.
elimination_hints: A incorrectly calls dynamic stretching 'less effective'; C ignores evidence against pre-activity static stretching; D denies well-documented differences.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: Which of the following is a correct example of an isotonic exercise that involves both concentric and eccentric muscle contractions during its execution?

options.text: Holding a heavy bag stationary above the head without any arm movement for thirty seconds
Performing a push-up where muscles shorten while pushing up (concentric) and lengthen while lowering down (eccentric)
Pressing both palms together in front of the chest as hard as possible without movement
Standing still in a squat position with knees bent at ninety degrees for one minute

explanation: A push-up is a classic isotonic exercise with both contraction phases: during the upward push, the chest and triceps muscles shorten (concentric contraction), and during the controlled lowering, they lengthen under tension (eccentric contraction). Options A, C and D are all isometric exercises (no joint movement).
elimination_hints: A (static hold), C (pressing palms without movement) and D (static squat hold) all lack joint movement — they are isometric, not isotonic.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: Analyze why the principle of specificity is important when selecting weight training exercises for a javelin thrower, and identify which exercise selection approach best applies this principle.

options.text: The javelin thrower should only perform leg exercises because throwing power comes exclusively from the legs

The javelin thrower should select exercises that mimic the throwing movement pattern, targeting the shoulder, core and hip muscles involved in javelin release, such as overhead presses, rotational core work and hip extension exercises
The specificity principle is irrelevant in weight training and any random exercise selection will produce identical results for the javelin thrower
The javelin thrower should avoid all upper-body exercises to prevent shoulder injuries

explanation: The specificity principle states that training adaptations are specific to the muscles and movement patterns trained. A javelin thrower must select exercises targeting the shoulder complex, rotational core muscles and hip extensors — the primary muscles and movement patterns used in javelin throwing. Overhead presses, rotational exercises and hip extensions closely mimic these demands.
elimination_hints: A ignores the crucial upper-body and core involvement; C denies the specificity principle; D prevents training the most important muscles for throwing.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: Which of the following correctly describes the recommended cool-down procedure that an athlete should follow immediately after completing a high-intensity training session?

options.text: Immediately lie down flat on the ground and remain completely motionless for ten minutes
Perform five to ten minutes of light aerobic activity such as slow jogging or walking, followed by static stretching of the major muscle groups used during the session
Take a cold-water bath immediately and skip all physical activity after the session ends
Perform another set of high-intensity sprints to ensure all energy stores are fully depleted

explanation: The recommended cool-down involves 5–10 minutes of light aerobic activity (slow jogging, walking) to gradually lower heart rate and aid blood redistribution, followed by static stretching of the major muscle groups to maintain flexibility and reduce post-exercise stiffness. Static stretching is appropriate during cool-down (unlike before explosive activity).
elimination_hints: A causes blood pooling (lying flat suddenly); C skips the gradual transition; D adds intensity instead of reducing it.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: Evaluate which of the following weight training principles is being violated if an athlete performs the same exercises with the same weight, same repetitions and same sets every week for six months without any modification to the programme.

options.text: The principle of warm-up is being violated because the athlete is not stretching before each session
The principle of progressive overload is being violated because the training stimulus is not being gradually increased, leading to a performance plateau
The principle of individuality is being violated because the programme was not designed for a different athlete
The principle of reversibility is being violated because the athlete has been training consistently without breaks

explanation: Performing identical exercises with the same load, reps and sets for six months directly violates progressive overload — the principle that training stimulus must be gradually increased for continued adaptation. Without increasing weight, reps, sets or complexity, the body has no reason to adapt further, causing a performance plateau.
elimination_hints: A is about warm-up, not programme design; C is about individual differences, not progression; D describes the opposite problem (reversibility occurs from stopping, not continuing).
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: A basketball player incorporates both isometric wall sits and isotonic squats into her strength programme. Analyze how each exercise type contributes differently to her on-court performance in basketball.

options.text: Both exercises produce identical performance benefits and there is no difference in their contribution to basketball skills

Isometric wall sits develop static holding strength useful for defensive stance maintenance, while isotonic squats develop dynamic leg power needed for jumping, sprinting and rapid direction changes during the game
Isometric wall sits improve vertical jump height more than squats because static contraction generates more explosive power
Isotonic squats are only useful for warming up and have no role in developing game-relevant strength

explanation: Isometric wall sits develop the ability to maintain a low defensive stance without movement (static strength), useful for guarding opponents. Isotonic squats develop dynamic leg strength and power through the full range of motion, translating directly to jumping, sprinting, landing and quick direction changes — all essential basketball movements.
elimination_hints: A ignores fundamental differences between static and dynamic training; C is physiologically incorrect (isometric does not develop explosive power); D undervalues squats.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: Match the following weight training principles in Column A with their correct definitions in Column B.

column_b.text: Training adaptations are lost when the training stimulus is removed or significantly reduced over time
Training must be tailored to the individual's fitness level, goals, age and physical characteristics
The training load must be gradually increased over time for continued improvement in strength
Training adaptations are specific to the type of exercise, movement pattern and muscles trained

explanation: Progressive overload = gradually increase load (R). Specificity = adaptations specific to exercise type (S). Reversibility = adaptations lost when training stops (P). Individuality = tailored to the individual (Q).
elimination_hints: Progressive overload (1) is about 'gradually increasing' — only R uses this language. Reversibility (3) is about 'loss' of adaptations — only P matches.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: Match the following warm-up or cool-down activity in Column A with the specific physiological purpose it primarily serves in Column B.

column_a.text: Light jogging for five minutes at the start of warm-up
Dynamic leg swings and arm circles during warm-up
Slow walking for five minutes after intense exercise
Static hamstring stretch held for thirty seconds during cool-down

column_b.text: Maintains muscle pump action to prevent blood pooling and gradually reduces heart rate
Increases core body temperature, heart rate and blood flow to working muscles
Improves flexibility and reduces post-exercise muscle tension and stiffness
Takes joints through their range of motion, activates neuromuscular pathways and improves coordination

explanation: Light jogging → raises temperature, HR, blood flow (Q). Dynamic swings/circles → joint ROM, neuromuscular activation (S). Slow walking post-exercise → prevents blood pooling, gradual HR reduction (P). Static hamstring stretch → flexibility, reduce muscle tension (R).
elimination_hints: Light jogging (1) is clearly about raising temperature and HR (Q); static stretching (4) is clearly about flexibility (R). Use these anchors.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: Match the following training methods or exercise types in Column A with the athlete or scenario for which they are most suitable in Column B.

column_a.text: Isotonic exercises with heavy loads and low repetitions
Weight training with moderate loads and high repetitions

column_b.text: A weightlifter preparing for a one-rep-max competition requiring maximal strength
A knee-injury patient who cannot move the joint but needs to maintain quadriceps strength
A school PE class of forty students with limited equipment and time
A boxer developing muscular endurance to sustain punching power through twelve rounds

explanation: Isometric → knee-injury patient (no joint movement needed, Q). Heavy loads/low reps → weightlifter for maximal strength (P). Circuit/bodyweight → school class with limited resources (R). Moderate loads/high reps → boxer for muscular endurance (S).
elimination_hints: Isometric (1) is ideal for injury rehab with no movement (Q); circuit with bodyweight (3) suits large school groups (R). Anchor with these.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below and select the correct option regarding the design of circuit training programmes.
assertion: In a well-designed circuit training programme, exercises targeting the same muscle group should not be placed at consecutive stations.
reason: Placing exercises for the same muscle group at consecutive stations causes premature local muscular fatigue, reducing exercise quality and increasing the risk of poor technique and injury.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

explanation: The Assertion is true — consecutive stations should target different muscle groups. The Reason correctly explains why: targeting the same muscles consecutively leads to premature local fatigue, degraded exercise quality, poor technique and increased injury risk. Alternating muscle groups allows recovery between stations.
elimination_hints: Both statements are established circuit training design principles. The Reason provides the specific physiological and safety rationale for the Assertion's design rule.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below and select the correct option regarding the role of warming up in sports performance.
assertion: A proper warm-up before competition can improve an athlete's performance by enhancing speed of muscle contraction and range of motion at the joints.
reason: Warming up decreases body temperature and slows down metabolic processes, which makes muscles contract more efficiently.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

explanation: The Assertion is true — warming up improves muscle contraction speed and joint range of motion. However, the Reason is false — warming up increases (not decreases) body temperature and accelerates (not slows) metabolic processes. The elevated temperature is what improves muscle function.
elimination_hints: The Reason claims warming up 'decreases body temperature' — this is the opposite of what warming up does by definition, making it clearly false.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below and select the correct option regarding weight training and bone health.
assertion: Regular weight training increases bone mineral density and reduces the risk of osteoporosis in later life.
reason: When muscles pull on bones during weight training exercises, the mechanical stress stimulates osteoblast activity, leading to increased bone formation and density.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

explanation: The Assertion is true — weight training increases bone mineral density and helps prevent osteoporosis. The Reason is also true and provides the correct mechanism: mechanical loading from weight training stimulates osteoblasts (bone-forming cells) through Wolff's Law, leading to increased bone formation and density.
elimination_hints: Both statements are well-supported by exercise physiology research. The Reason explains the precise cellular mechanism (osteoblast stimulation) behind the Assertion's claim.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: Arrange the following weight training programme variables in the logical order a coach would determine them when designing a new strength programme for a beginner athlete.
items: I. Determine the number of sets and rest periods between sets based on the training goal
II. Assess the athlete's current fitness level, health status and training history
III. Select appropriate exercises based on the athlete's sport demands and movement patterns
IV. Determine the training load (weight) and number of repetitions for each exercise

options.text: II → III → IV → I
IV → I → III → II
I → IV → II → III
III → II → I → IV

explanation: Logical sequence: Assess fitness level and history (II, baseline assessment) → Select exercises based on sport demands (III, exercise choice) → Determine load and reps (IV, intensity prescription) → Determine sets and rest periods (I, volume and recovery). Assessment precedes all decisions; volume/rest is set last after intensity is decided.
elimination_hints: Assessment (II) always comes first in programme design; sets/rest (I) is the final calibration step. Anchor with these endpoints.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: Arrange the following phases of a complete training session in the correct chronological order from beginning to end as recommended by standard Physical Education guidelines.
items: I. Cool-down phase with light activity and static stretching
II. Warm-up phase with general and specific activities
III. Main activity phase (skill practice, game play, or conditioning exercises)
IV. Brief hydration and mental preparation between warm-up and main activity

options.text: II → IV → III → I
III → I → II → IV
I → II → IV → III
IV → III → II → I

explanation: Standard session order: Warm-up (II) → Brief hydration and mental preparation (IV) → Main activity (III) → Cool-down (I). The warm-up prepares the body, a brief transition allows hydration and focus, the main activity delivers the primary training stimulus, and the cool-down facilitates recovery.
elimination_hints: Warm-up (II) always starts the session; cool-down (I) always ends it. These anchor the first and last positions.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: True or False: Cooling down after exercise is also known as 'limbering down' and it involves gradually reducing the intensity of physical activity rather than stopping suddenly.

options.text: True
False

explanation: This statement is True. Cooling down is also called 'limbering down' in Physical Education terminology. It involves gradually reducing exercise intensity (e.g., from running to jogging to walking) rather than stopping abruptly, which helps the body transition safely from exercise to rest.
elimination_hints: 'Limbering down' is a standard synonym for cooling down in PE textbooks, and the gradual reduction principle is the core concept.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE

question_text: Study the diagram below that shows six common exercises classified by their type (isometric or isotonic) along with the primary muscles targeted and their suitability for rehabilitation. Based on the diagram, which exercise would a physiotherapist most likely prescribe first for a patient with a recently operated anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) of the knee who cannot yet bend the joint?

options.text: Barbell squat — because it is the most effective exercise for overall leg strength development
Straight-leg quadriceps isometric hold — because it activates the quadriceps without requiring any knee joint movement, which is critical when the healing ACL cannot be stressed through bending
Jumping lunges — because explosive movements accelerate ACL healing and recovery
Deadlift — because it develops posterior chain strength needed for knee stability

explanation: After ACL surgery, the knee joint cannot be moved through its range initially. A straight-leg quadriceps isometric hold activates and strengthens the quadriceps (essential for knee stability) without requiring any knee bending or ACL stress. Barbell squats, jumping lunges and deadlifts all require significant knee movement and loading that would be unsafe in early ACL rehabilitation.
elimination_hints: ACL surgery restricts knee movement — any exercise requiring bending (A, C, D) is inappropriate in early rehab. Only B (isometric, no movement) is safe.
subject: Physical Education
chapter: Sociological Aspects, Training & Career in PE
